workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Cable Middle Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered stance (one foot slightly forward) with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip and extend your arms out to the sides so there is tension on the cables; keep a small, constant bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades and keep the shoulders down; brace your core to prevent torso movement.
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion with control, allowing the arms to return to the starting position until you feel a gentle stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding torso leaning, shoulder shrugging, or locking the elbows.
exercises.description: Standing bilateral cable fly performed with the pulleys set at mid-chest height; the exercise horizontally adducts the arms in a controlled arc to load and contract the pectorals. A staggered stance and slight elbow bend are used to stabilize and protect the joints while the chest performs the work.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level when seated and your back rests flat against the pad.
Select an appropriate weight and sit upright with feet flat on the floor, chest up and shoulders down and back.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and position your upper arms roughly parallel to the floor with a small bend in the elbows.
Start with the handles out to the sides so your chest is stretched but shoulders are not overstretched; maintain a stable torso against the back pad.
Exhale and drive the handles together in a smooth horizontal arc by contracting the pectorals, bringing your hands close together or slightly in front of the chest.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, squeezing the chest while keeping elbows slightly bent and avoiding shoulder shrugging.
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to return to the start position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding leaning forward or using momentum to move the weight.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly that brings the arms together in a horizontal arc to isolate and contract the pectoral muscles. Performed on a lever/pec-deck machine with the chest supported and elbows slightly bent throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

exercises.name: Cable Upper Chest Crossovers
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (above shoulder height) and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to a high position (above shoulder height) and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the stations with a staggered or shoulder-width stance for stability and grab a handle in each hand (palms facing down or slightly rotated), arms out to the sides.
Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso is upright but leaning a bit toward the floor; keep chest up, shoulders down and back, and a slight bend in the elbows throughout.

Exhale and pull both handles in a controlled downward and inward arc, bringing your hands together in front of the upper chest/clavicle area (hands may cross slightly) while actively squeezing the pectorals.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding shoulder rotation or shrugging.
Inhale and slowly return the handles along the same arc back to the start position with control, resisting the weight rather than letting it pull you back.
Keep core engaged and avoid using momentum; choose a weight that allows full range of motion with good form.
exercises.description: Standing cable crossovers performed with the pulleys set high to emphasize the upper chest; the movement brings the handles down and together in a downward arc to target the clavicular portion of the pectorals. Maintain a slight forward lean and a small bend in the elbows while squeezing the chest at the finish.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Around Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your head near one end, feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your head near one end, feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips.

Grasp one dumbbell vertically with both hands (palms under the top plate or hold the handles together) and extend your arms so the weight is directly over your sternum; maintain a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the bench to avoid excessive lumbar extension throughout the movement.

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arcing path backward and overhead, keeping the same slight elbow bend and letting the chest and lats stretch; stop when you feel a comfortable stretch or when the dumbbell is just below head level.
Without pausing, continue the arc so the dumbbell moves down behind your head and then back around toward the starting position over the chest, completing the circular 'around' path.
Exhale as you pull the weight back over the chest, using the chest and lats to drive the movement; keep shoulders down and avoid shrugging.
Repeat for the desired reps with smooth, controlled tempo; reduce range or weight if you experience shoulder discomfort.
Maintain neutral neck position and steady breathing (inhale on the lowering/stretch, exhale on the return).
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell pullover performed with an arcing 'around' motion that moves the weight from over the chest back and overhead, emphasizing chest stretch and ribcage/lat engagement. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and perform a controlled, continuous arc without shrugging or arching the lower back.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
instructions: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
Stand facing across from the pulley in a staggered stance (one foot forward for balance) with a slight forward lean at the hips; engage your core and keep a neutral spine.
Reach up and grab the handle with the working hand using a neutral grip; allow the arm to start out to the side with a small bend in the elbow.
Depress and stabilize your shoulder (avoid shrugging) and pull the handle down and across your body in a smooth arc toward the opposite mid-chest, rotating the torso slightly as needed.
At the end of the movement, actively squeeze the pectorals and serratus anterior while maintaining the elbow bend; pause briefly at peak contraction.
Slowly and under control, return the handle to the starting position allowing the chest to stretch but keeping tension on the cable; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Keep breathing steadily (exhale as you pull across, inhale as you return).
Maintain a slight elbow bend throughout, avoid shoulder hyperextension, and do not use excessive torso rotation or momentum to drive the movement.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable crossover isolates one side of the chest by pulling a cable across the body from a high pulley, emphasizing the pectoral contraction and allowing a strong end-range squeeze. It is performed standing with a slight forward lean and a controlled return to the start position.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
instructions: Sit on the bench/seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; select an appropriate weight.
Lean forward from the hips and reach to grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders rounded forward (scapulae protracted).
Keep a neutral spine and a slight chest-up posture; initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades (pull shoulders down and back) before bending the elbows.

Pull the handle toward your torso (near the lower chest/upper abdomen) driving the elbows back and keeping them close to your sides; squeeze the shoulder blades together at the end of the concentric phase.
Pause briefly at full contraction, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding excessive torso swing or leaning back.
Slowly extend the arms and allow the shoulder blades to protract as you return to the starting position with control, keeping tension on the cable.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine, controlled tempo, and full scapular retraction on each rep.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed from a seated position that primarily targets the latissimus dorsi while working the mid/upper back and arms. It develops scapular retraction and elbow flexion strength through a controlled pull and release.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Bar Lateral Pulldown
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the lat pulldown bench with feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
instructions: Sit upright on the lat pulldown bench with feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
Reach up and take a wide, pronated (overhand) grip on the cable bar, hands wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with chest up and a slight natural arch in the lower back; lean back slightly at the hips (about 10–20 degrees) to create a stable pulling angle.
Exhale and pull the bar straight down toward the upper chest, driving the elbows down and back and leading the movement with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom of the movement, keeping the shoulders down and avoiding shrugging.
Pause briefly (about 1 second) at the bottom while maintaining tension, then inhale and slowly return the bar to the fully extended starting position with controlled resistance.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while avoiding excessive torso swing or momentum and keeping the wrists neutral.
exercises.description: Seated cable bar lateral pulldown using a wide pronated grip to pull the bar down toward the upper chest, emphasizing vertical adduction of the arms to load the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Performed seated with knees braced under the pads and a controlled, full range of motion.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely on the floor.
Select a pair of dumbbells and sit at the end of the bench, then carefully lie prone with your chest supported on the bench and feet on the floor or toes braced for stability.
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a neutral or palms-facing-in grip on the dumbbells; maintain a neutral neck and spine.
Engage your core and pull your shoulder blades back and down to initiate the movement.
Drive your elbows up and back, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs/waist while keeping elbows close to the body and squeezing the upper back at the top.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular retraction and avoid shrugging the shoulders toward the ears.
Lower the dumbbells slowly under control to the starting position until the arms are nearly extended, keeping tension in the back throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the concentric (pull) and breathing in on the eccentric (lower).
Keep movements controlled, avoid torso rotation or using momentum, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain scapular control.
exercises.description: A prone row performed on an incline bench where the chest is supported and dumbbells are pulled toward the torso to emphasize the upper back. It uses a controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the mid/upper back and pulling musculature.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet planted on the floor and your torso flat against the bench (supine position).
Grasp a barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart and lift it straight up over your chest with elbows bent roughly 90 degrees; keep the elbows fixed throughout the movement.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the shoulders before beginning the descent.

Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing shoulder extension (keeping the elbow angle constant) until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest area or until the bar reaches a comfortable range of motion.

Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the lats and serratus anterior, then pull the bar back over the chest by driving the shoulders into flexion using your lats and chest (exhale on the concentric).
Keep the movement smooth and controlled, avoiding excessive lower back arch; maintain the bent-elbow position and avoid turning the elbows outward or using momentum.
Perform the desired number of reps and then safely rack the barbell, or have a spotter assist when lowering the weight off your chest.
exercises.description: A supine barbell pullover performed with the elbows bent that moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, stretching and loading the latissimus region while also recruiting the chest and triceps. Performed with a fixed elbow angle to emphasize shoulder extension and thoracic mobility.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Hold a loaded barbell with a shoulder-width, pronated (overhand) grip so the bar rests behind your thighs at arm's length.
Keep arms straight (no elbow bend) and head/neck in a neutral position throughout the set.
Elevate the shoulders straight up toward the ears by contracting the traps; avoid rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Pause and squeeze the top of the contraction for 1–2 seconds, focusing on the traps.
Lower the shoulders back down slowly and under control until arms are fully extended and traps are relaxed.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining upright posture and minimal torso movement.
exercises.description: Standing barbell shrugs isolate the trapezius by elevating the shoulders while holding a loaded barbell. The movement focuses on a vertical shrugging motion with minimal elbow or torso movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to the low position and select an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat with feet braced on the foot platform and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the low position and select an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat with feet braced on the foot platform and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the handle with both hands using a neutral or overhand grip, then sit upright with a neutral spine and chest lifted; brace your abs.
Start with your arms extended and shoulders relaxed forward (the starting position).
Exhale and pull the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together — keep elbows close to your body.
Avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum; maintain a stable, upright torso (a small, controlled lean is acceptable but do not rock).
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while maintaining scapular retraction and a tight core.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms to return the handle to the start position with control, allowing the shoulders to protract but not round excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping movements smooth and controlled; lower the weight if you find yourself shrugging or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated low cable row is a horizontal-pulling exercise performed from a seated position that primarily develops the lats while also engaging the mid- and upper-back and arm muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle toward the torso.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Cable Close Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads sit snugly over your thighs to prevent you from rising during the pull.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads sit snugly over your thighs to prevent you from rising during the pull.

Select an appropriate weight and attach a close-grip (V-bar or narrow neutral) handle. Sit upright on the bench facing the machine and grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated close grip.
Start with your arms fully extended overhead, chest up, shoulders down and back, and a slight natural arch in the lower back; lean back only a few degrees (not a full lean).
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down toward your sides and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together and pulling the handle to the upper chest or clavicle area.
Pause for 1 second at the bottom while maintaining tension in the lats and keeping the torso stable (avoid excessive swinging or using momentum).
Slowly extend your arms back to the start under control until elbows are nearly straight, feeling a stretch in the lats, then repeat for the desired reps.
Keep breathing steadily (exhale during the pull, inhale during the return) and avoid shrugging the shoulders or letting the wrists bend excessively to protect the traps and forearms.
exercises.description: A seated cable pulldown using a close/narrow grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi while reducing wide-grip involvement; performed by pulling the handle to the upper chest with elbows driving down and back. Useful for building lower and mid-lat thickness with increased biceps and grip involvement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip, palms facing the body).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip, palms facing the body).
Keep a neutral spine and neck, chest up, and arms straight but not locked; maintain a slight bend in the knees.
Exhale and lift your shoulders straight upward toward your ears — move only the shoulders, not the arms or torso.
Pause and squeeze the top of the traps for 1–2 seconds while keeping the neck relaxed (do not jut the chin forward).
Inhale and lower the shoulders back down under control to the starting position; avoid letting the weights drop.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining slow, controlled reps and avoiding any rolling or circular shoulder motion.
If grip is limiting, consider using lifting straps; for variation you can perform seated shrugs to reduce lower body involvement.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell shrugs raise the shoulders straight up toward the ears to primarily target the trapezius and supporting shoulder/neck muscles. Use a controlled motion with a brief squeeze at the top and avoid rolling the shoulders.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart and knees unlocked (soft bend).
instructions: Stand upright (neutral spine) with feet about hip-width apart and knees unlocked (soft bend).
Grip a barbell with both hands, arms fully extended, using an overhand (pronated) or mixed grip; hold the bar at thigh level in front of your body.
Keep your torso tall, chest up and head neutral — avoid leaning or using lower back to lift.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears by contracting the traps and levator scapulae; do not rotate or roll the shoulders.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction while keeping arms straight and elbows relaxed.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start, controlling the descent through the full range.
Repeat for the desired reps; maintain a steady tempo, avoid using momentum, and keep a firm grip (forearms engaged) throughout.
exercises.description: Standing barbell shrugs elevate the shoulders to target the upper trapezius and surrounding scapular elevators; the movement is a vertical scapular elevation with the arms kept straight. Perform controlled shrugs up toward the ears, pause at the top, then lower slowly to the start.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Squat
exercises.notes: Set a barbell in a squat rack at about upper-chest/shoulder height and load appropriate weight; use safety pins or a spotter if needed.
instructions: Set a barbell in a squat rack at about upper-chest/shoulder height and load appropriate weight; use safety pins or a spotter if needed.
Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper back/traps (not on your neck); grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Step back from the rack and take a wide stance (wider than shoulder-width) with toes pointed slightly outward (~30 degrees).
Brace your core, keep chest up and spine neutral, and ensure knees are tracking in the same direction as your toes.
Inhale, push your hips back slightly and bend at the hips and knees to lower your body until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor (or lower if mobility allows).
Keep weight distributed through your heels and midfoot; avoid letting the knees cave inward.
Exhale and drive through the heels to extend the hips and knees, returning to the starting standing position while maintaining an upright torso.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions and carefully re-rack the bar when finished.
exercises.description: A wide-stance barbell squat performed with the bar across the upper back; the wider stance and outward foot angle place extra emphasis on the inner thighs and quadriceps while also recruiting the glutes and hamstrings. Lower by bending hips and knees until the thighs are about parallel, then drive back up through the heels.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the platform and heels hanging off the back; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand tall (upright torso).
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the platform and heels hanging off the back; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand tall (upright torso).
Position feet about hip-width apart and keep a slight, natural bend in the knees (do not lock the knees).
Engage your abs to stabilize the torso and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise your heels by pressing through the balls of your feet until you are fully on your toes and calves are fully contracted; avoid rocking the torso forward.
Pause for 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the step level until you feel a stretch in the calves (control the descent — do not drop abruptly).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping movements smooth and maintaining balance; if needed, reduce weight or use a rail/wall for light support.
exercises.description: Standing calf raise performed on a raised step while holding dumbbells at the sides to load the lower leg; the movement raises the heels by plantarflexing the ankles to target the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves). Emphasis is on controlled concentric raise and slow eccentric lowering below the step for stretch.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell High Bar Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about upper-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about upper-chest height. Step under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar position), not on the neck.
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width and retract your shoulder blades to create a stable shelf; lift the bar off the rack and take 1–2 small steps back to clear the rack.
Stand with feet approximately shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward. Brace your abs and keep a tall, upright torso.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, keeping weight distributed through the midfoot to heels.
Descend under control until your thighs are approximately parallel to the floor (or slightly below if mobility allows), ensuring knees track over the toes and do not collapse inward.
Maintain chest up and a neutral spine throughout the descent; keep the upper back tight to support the bar.
Drive through the heels and midfoot, extend the knees and hips simultaneously to return to a standing position, exhaling as you rise.
Reset your brace at the top, then repeat for the desired number of repetitions. When finished, step forward into the rack and safely re-rack the bar.
exercises.description: A barbell high bar squat places the bar on the upper traps and emphasizes knee flexion to target the quads while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings. The lifter descends to at least parallel with an upright torso, then drives back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Leg Diagonal Kickback
exercises.notes: Attach an ankle cuff to a low cable pulley and secure it around the working ankle.
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to a low cable pulley and secure it around the working ankle.

Stand upright facing the machine with the non-working foot planted on the floor (or on a small balance pad) and a slight bend in that knee; grasp the machine or hold a handle with one hand for light support and stabilization.
Shift your weight onto the planted leg, brace your abs to keep the torso stable and upright, and allow the working leg to hang straight behind you with toes pointed or slightly flexed.

Without bending the working knee, drive the leg straight back and diagonally across your body (slightly outward and behind), contracting the glute at the top of the movement; avoid arching the lower back or leaning the torso forward.
Pause briefly at full extension while squeezing the glute, then slowly return the leg to the starting position under control without letting the cable pull your torso out of alignment.
Perform the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides and repeat.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-leg diagonal kickbacks use a low-pulley ankle attachment to extend a straight working leg backward and slightly across the midline, emphasizing hip extension and glute contraction while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Sled 45 degrees Leg Press
exercises.notes: Set up: Sit and then lie supine on the sled bench which is angled at 45 degrees; place your feet shoulder-width on the middle of the footplate with toes pointing slightly outward.
instructions: Set up: Sit and then lie supine on the sled bench which is angled at 45 degrees; place your feet shoulder-width on the middle of the footplate with toes pointing slightly outward.
Grip the side handles and ensure your lower back and hips are pressed firmly against the backpad; unlock the sled safety/locks if present.
Start position: Bend your knees to bring your thighs toward your chest until knees are about 90 degrees, keeping feet flat on the plate and knees tracking over toes.
Press: Exhale and drive through your heels and mid-foot to extend your knees and hips, pushing the footplate away until legs are nearly straight but do not lock the knees at full extension.
Return: Inhale and slowly control the sled back down by bending the knees, lowering the weight under tension until your knees reach about 90 degrees again.
Repetitions: Repeat for the desired number of reps with controlled tempo; avoid bouncing at the bottom or using momentum to lock the knees.
Safety cues: Keep your lower back pressed into the pad throughout, avoid lifting hips off the pad, and keep knees aligned with feet. Rerack/lock the sled before exiting.
exercises.description: A 45-degree sled leg press performed supine on an inclined sled platform where you push the footplate away using the legs. The movement primarily extends the knees to target the quadriceps while the glutes, hamstrings, and calves assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright on the machine platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; position your shoulders under the padded lever and grasp the handles.
instructions: Stand upright on the machine platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; position your shoulders under the padded lever and grasp the handles.
Unlatch or release the safety so the weight rests on the shoulders and stand tall with a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (not locked).
Inhale, then exhale as you press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, squeezing the calves at the top.
Hold the top position briefly (1 second) while maintaining an upright torso and stable hips.
Inhale as you slowly lower your heels down below the platform level until you feel a stretch in the calves.
Control the descent—avoid bouncing—and repeat for desired reps, then carefully re-rack or secure the lever when finished.
exercises.description: A bilateral standing calf raise performed on a lever/shoulder machine that isolates the calf muscles through plantarflexion. The exercise raises the heels off a platform, then lowers them below platform level for a full stretch.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Straight Legs Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand, arms straight and palms facing the body.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and a dumbbell in each hand, arms straight and palms facing the body.
Soften the knees slightly (do not lock them) and set a neutral spine by bracing the abs and pulling the shoulder blades back slightly.
Initiate the movement by pushing the hips straight back (hip hinge), keeping the dumbbells close to the front of the thighs.
Lower the dumbbells down the front of your legs until you feel a strong but controlled stretch in the hamstrings (around mid-shin or just below the knees), maintaining a flat back and neutral neck.
Keep the knees only slightly bent throughout; avoid excessive knee bend or letting the lower back round.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then drive the hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the dumbbells close to the legs.
Maintain a firm grip so the forearms remain engaged, exhale as you stand, and repeat for the desired reps.
Use lighter weights or reduce range of motion if you cannot maintain a neutral spine or hamstring tension throughout the set.
exercises.description: A hip-hinge movement performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric and concentric loading of the hamstrings while also engaging the glutes and lower back; performed with nearly straight legs and a neutral spine. Keep the weights close to the legs and drive the hips back and forward to move the load.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades slightly back and down to engage the upper back, and keep a neutral (flat) spine throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart, toes pointing forward, a dumbbell in each hand held at arm's length at your sides (or in front of thighs) with a neutral grip.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades slightly back and down to engage the upper back, and keep a neutral (flat) spine throughout the movement.
Slightly bend the knees (soft knees) — do not squat — and hinge at the hips by pushing them back while keeping the chest open and eyes forward or slightly down.
Lower the dumbbells in a straight path close to your legs until they reach about mid-shin (or you feel a deep stretch in the hamstrings) while maintaining the neutral spine.
Drive through the heels and contract the glutes and hamstrings to extend the hips and return to a tall standing position; squeeze the glutes at the top without hyperextending the lower back.
Keep the dumbbells close to the body during the entire movement and maintain even breathing (inhale on the descent, exhale as you stand).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining form; reduce range of motion or weight if you cannot keep a neutral spine.
Common faults to avoid: rounding the lower back, letting the knees collapse inward, using the arms to lift (should be hip-driven), and hyperextending the lumbar spine at the top.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge strength exercise using dumbbells to develop the posterior chain; it emphasizes hip extension to drive the body upright while maintaining a neutral spine. Performed from a standing position with a slight knee bend, lowering the weights toward the shins and then driving the hips forward to return to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Press (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load appropriate plates on the machine's weight pegs and ensure the safety latch/lock is engaged before sitting.
instructions: Load appropriate plates on the machine's weight pegs and ensure the safety latch/lock is engaged before sitting.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the backrest, feet placed about shoulder-width apart on the center of the footplate (toes slightly pointed forward), and grip the side handles.
Disengage the safety/lock or release the lever according to the machine's design so the footplate can move freely.
Inhale and slowly bend your knees to lower the footplate until your knees are about 90 degrees (or a comfortable depth) while keeping your lower back pressed into the pad and avoiding hip rise.
Exhale and drive through your heels and mid-foot to extend your legs, straightening the knees until nearly extended but do not lock the knees at the top.
Control the return by slowly bending the knees to repeat the next rep; maintain steady tempo and full contact of your back with the pad throughout.
After the final repetition, re-engage the safety/lock before removing your feet from the footplate and unloading plates if necessary.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever leg press where you push a footplate away using your legs while your back is supported on an angled seat. Primarily develops knee extension strength (quadriceps) with assistance from the glutes, hamstrings and calves.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright in a split stance with one foot forward and the other foot stepped back (rear foot on the toes).
instructions: Stand upright in a split stance with one foot forward and the other foot stepped back (rear foot on the toes). Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, arms straight and palms facing the body.
Brace your core and keep your torso tall and neutral (avoid excessive forward lean). Position feet so that when you lower, the front knee tracks over the front toes without collapsing inward.

Begin the descent by bending both knees, lowering your hips straight down until the front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor or the front knee reaches about 90 degrees. Allow the rear knee to move toward the floor but not to rest on it.
Keep weight distributed through the front heel and midfoot; maintain even balance and a stable pelvis throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining an upright torso and engaged core.
Drive through the front heel to straighten the front leg and return to the starting upright split stance. Keep the movement controlled; do not lock the knee at the top.
Repeat for desired repetitions, then switch legs and perform the same number on the opposite side.
exercises.description: A single-leg, stationary lunge performed with dumbbells to load the front leg; it emphasizes the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings for hip stability. The movement is done in a split stance with a controlled descent and drive through the front heel to return to standing.

exercises.name: Sled Calf Press On Leg Press
exercises.notes: Set up the leg press sled with an appropriate weight and unlock the safety catches.
instructions: Set up the leg press sled with an appropriate weight and unlock the safety catches.
Sit reclined with your back and head firmly against the pad (supine/reclined position) and place the balls of your feet and toes on the lower edge of the footplate so your heels can hang freely.
Keep your knees slightly bent (do not lock) and grip the handles or sides of the seat for stability.
Inhale, then exhale and press through the balls of your feet to lift the sled by plantarflexing your ankles, raising your heels as high as comfortable.
Pause briefly at the top for a full contraction of the calves.
Slowly lower your heels back down below the level of the footplate to achieve a controlled stretch of the calves, keeping the motion smooth and under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then carefully re-engage the safety catches before exiting the machine.
exercises.description: Performed on a leg press sled while reclined, this exercise isolates ankle plantarflexion by pressing through the balls of the feet to raise the sled using the calf muscles. It emphasizes a full range of motion from a stretch (heels lowered) to a strong contraction (heels lifted).

exercises.name: Lever Lying Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area.
instructions: Set the lever machine so the pivot aligns with the knee joint and place the lower-leg pad just above the heels / over the Achilles area.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your hips flat on the pad, chest supported, and hands holding the front handles or bench for stability.
Position your legs fully extended with the pad resting on the back of your lower legs and feet relaxed; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Exhale and curl your lower legs by flexing the knees, bringing your heels toward your glutes in a controlled motion until you reach near-full knee flexion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top for 1 second, keeping hips and torso pressed into the bench (avoid lifting the hips).
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start (near full knee extension) under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining steady tempo and full range of motion; adjust load so you can control both concentric and eccentric phases.
Stop or reduce weight if you feel excessive hip rise, lower-back strain, or knee pain; keep movements smooth and joint-focused.
exercises.description: Prone (lying face down) leg curl performed on a lever machine that isolates the hamstrings by flexing the knee against a padded lever. The movement curls the lower leg toward the buttocks while the torso remains stabilized on the bench.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
Set a slight bend in the knees (soft knees) and brace your core to stabilize the spine before initiating the movement.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly lifted; allow the dumbbells to slide down the front of your thighs and shins, staying close to the body.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings or the dumbbells reach mid‑shin (maintain neutral cervical alignment, do not round the lower back).
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward, squeezing the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the knees softly bent and the weights close to the legs.
Maintain controlled tempo (slow on the descent, controlled drive on the ascent), keep the shoulders down away from the ears, and avoid using the lower back to lift.
exercises.description: A standing hip‑hinge exercise performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric loading of the hamstrings while the hips move back and forth; the movement also engages the glutes and core to maintain a neutral spine. Keep a slight knee bend and the weights close to the legs throughout.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set up a squat rack at an appropriate height and load the barbell; unrack the bar and place it securely across your upper back/traps (not on your neck).
instructions: Set up a squat rack at an appropriate height and load the barbell; unrack the bar and place it securely across your upper back/traps (not on your neck).

Step onto a raised, stable platform or aerobic step so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can drop freely; stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width and toes pointing forward.
Grip the bar with a comfortable overhand grip to maintain control; engage your abs and keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout (standing position).
Keep knees straight but not locked (a slight soft bend) and maintain weight balanced over the balls of your feet.
Exhale and raise your heels by plantarflexing at the ankle, lifting as high as possible onto your toes and pausing 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the platform level until you feel a stretch in the calves; control the descent — do not drop quickly or bounce.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and full range of motion. Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack if using heavy weight.
When finished, carefully step down from the platform and re-rack the bar in the squat rack.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raise performed from a raised platform with a barbell across the upper back; rise onto the toes to concentrically contract the calves, then lower the heels below the platform for a full stretch. This is a standing, weight-bearing isolation exercise for the calves with upper-back and core stabilization.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
